content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

May 17, 1990


Peter Bentley
Carpinterians Against Rampant Development,
A Save Our City Committee
Box 911
Carpinteria, CA 93013

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-90-265

Dear Mr. Bentley:

You have requested advice on behalf of Carpinterians Against
Rampant Development (CARD) and Save Our City (SOC) concerning the
campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ On
April 20, 1990, and May 2, 1990, I spoke with you and received
some additional information.

FACTS

During our telephone conversations, you stated that Save Our
City is the organization referred to in your letter and CARD is
the committee, that you are one of four directors for SOC, and
that you are authorized to request advice on behalf of SOC as well
as CARD. You also said that SOC was formed about a year ago to
educate residents of Carpinteria and the surrounding area about
issues related to the quality of life, that CARD was formed to
oppose a local ballot measure, and that CARD does not make
contributions to candidates. In addition, you stated that SOC
receives its funds from donations, but it has never made political
contributions and has not solicited or received any contributions
to support or oppose candidates or ballot measures. SOC is now
considering making contributions to CARD.

QUESTIONS

1. May SOC make unlimited contributions to CARD without
disclosing the sources of those funds?

___________________
¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Bentley, Peter
Page 2

2. May SOC make contributions to candidates, subject to
applicable contribution limits, without disclosure of the sources
of those funds?

3. May CARD or SOC receive non-monetary contributions?

CONCLUSIONS

1. Assuming that SOC has not received and does not receive
contributions totalling $1,000 or more in a calendar year for
political purposes, it may make unlimited contributions to CARD
during 1990 and will not be required to disclose contributions it
receives. However, if SOC makes contributions totalling $10,000
or more in a calendar year to candidates or committees (including
CARD), it will qualify as a "major donor" committee and must file
reports disclosing the contributions that it makes. If in any
subsequent year SOC makes contributions to California state or
local candidates or committees totalling $1,000 or more, it will
qualify as a "recipient" committee and must file reports
disclosing contributions it receives as well as contributions that
it makes.

2. Assuming that SOC has not received and does not receive
contributions totalling $1,000 or more in a calendar year for
political purposes, it may make contributions to candidates during
1990 and will not be required to disclose the contributions it
receives. However, if SOC makes contributions totalling $10,000
or more in a calendar year to candidates or committees (including
CARD), it will qualify as a "major donor" committee and must file
reports disclosing the contributions that it makes. If in any
subsequent year SOC makes contributions to California state or
local candidates or committees totalling $1,000 or more, it will
qualify as a "recipient" committee and must file reports
disclosing contributions it receives as well as contributions that
it makes.

3. CARD or SOC may receive non-monetary contributions.
However, if SOC receives monetary or non-monetary contributions
for political purposes aggregating $1,000 or more during a
calendar year, it will qualify as a "recipient" committee and must
file reports disclosing contributions received and expenditures
made.

ANALYSIS

1. and 2. Proposition 73 imposed limits on contributions made
to candidates and to committees which make contributions to

Bentley, Peter
Page 3

candidates.² (Sections 85301-85303.) The Act does not limit
contributions to committees which are formed for purposes other
than making contributions to candidates, such as committees formed
to support or oppose ballot measures.

Section 82015 defines "contribution" as:

...a payment, a forgiveness of a loan, a payment of a loan by
a third party, or an enforceable promise to make a payment
except to the extent that full and adequate consideration is
received unless it is clear from the surrounding
circumstances that it is not made for political purposes.
An expenditure made at the behest of a candidate, committee
or elected officer is a contribution to the candidate,
committee or elected officer unless full and adequate
consideration is received for making the expenditure.

Regulation 18215 (copy enclosed) clarifies that a payment is
made for "political purposes" if it is:

(1) For the purpose of influencing or attempting
to influence the action of the voters for or against the
nomination or election of a candidate or candidates, or
the qualification or passage of any measure; or

(2) Received by or made at the behest of:

_________________________

² The Act limits contributions which a candidate may receive
from a "person" to $1,000 per fiscal year (July 1 - June 30). A
"person" includes an individual, business entity, or other entity
which does not receive contributions for political purposes.
Contributions from a "political committee" to a candidate are
limited to $2,500 per fiscal year, and contributions from a
political party or a "broad based political committee" to a
candidate are limited to $5,000 per fiscal year. Contributions to
"political committees" and "broad based political committee" are
limited to $2,500 per fiscal year from any source. A "political
committee" is a group of persons that receives contributions from
two or more persons and makes contributions to candidates.
(Sections 85102(c).) A "broad based political committee" is a
committee of persons which has been in existence for more than six
months, receives contributions from one hundred or more persons,
and acting in concert makes contributions to five or more
candidates. (Section 85102(d).) Enclosed is an information
manual which summarizes these provisions.

Bentley, Peter
Page 4

(A) A candidate, unless it is clear from
surrounding circumstances that the payment was
received or made at his or her behest for personal
purposes unrelated to his or her candidacy or
status as an office holder. The term "payment"
includes the candidate's own money or property used
on behalf of his or her candidacy;

(B) A controlled committee;

(C) An official committee of a political
party, including a state central committee, county
central committee, assembly district committee or
any subcommittee of such committee; or

(D) An organization formed or existing
primarily for political purposes as defined in
subsection (a)(1), including but not limited to a
political action committee established by any
membership organization, labor union or
corporation.

(b) "Made at the behest" means a payment made
under the control or at the direction of, in
cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert
with, or at the request or suggestion of a candidate,
controlled committee, official committee of a political
party, or organization formed or existing primarily for
political purposes.

Section 82013 defines three types of "committees" which must
file periodic campaign disclosure reports.

"Committee" means any person or combination of persons
who directly or indirectly does any of the following:

(a) Receives contributions totaling one thousand
dollars ($1,000) or more in a calendar year;

(b) Makes independent expenditures totaling one
thousand dollars ($1,000) or more in a calendar year; or

(c) Makes contributions totaling ten thousand dollars
($10,000) or more in a calendar year to or at the behest of
candidates or committees....

You have indicated that although SOC is funded by donations,
it has not solicited or received contributions specifically for
the purpose of making contributions or expenditures to support or
oppose candidates or ballot measures, and has not to date used any
of the funds it receives for that purpose. Therefore, SOC has not
qualified as a "recipient" committee pursuant to Section 82013(a).

Bentley, Peter
Page 5

However, SOC will qualify as a recipient committee if it
solicits or receives contributions in the future for the purpose
of supporting or opposing candidates or ballot measures. In
addition, non-political donations received by SOC will meet the
Act's definition of "contribution" and SOC will qualify as a
recipient committee if SOC uses those donations for political
purposes in more than one calendar year. Regulation 18215
provides:

(c) "Contribution" includes any monetary or non-
monetary payment for which full and adequate consideration
is not made to the donor that is received by any
person or organization other than a candidate,
controlled committee, official committee of a political
party, or organization formed or existing primarily for
political purposes if the payment is "earmarked" for the
making of contribution or expenditures. A payment is
"earmarked" when, at the time of making the payment, the
donor knows or has reason to know that the payment or
funds with which the payment will be commingled will be
used to make contributions, as defined in Government
Code Section 82015 and this regulation, or expenditures,
as defined in Government Code Section 82025 and 2 Cal.
Adm. Code Section 18225. If the donor knows or has
reason to know that only part of the payment will be
used to make contributions or expenditures, the payment
shall be apportioned on a reasonable basis in order to
determine the amount of contribution. Factors relevant
to a determination that the donor has reason to know
that all or part of the payment will be used to make
expenditures and contributions include but are not
limited to the established practice of the person or
organization with respect to expenditures and
contributions and any representations made when the
payment is solicited.

The Commission has advised that an organization which uses
membership dues or other non-political donations to support or
oppose candidates or ballot measures, and which has not notified
its members that it will use the funds to make contributions or
expenditures, will qualify as a recipient committee under Section
82013(a) during the second year in which it engages in such
activities. That is because any contributions and expenditures
made during the first calendar year in which the organization
engages in such activities establish a "history" that puts the
organization's donors on notice that some or all of their funds
may be used for political purposes. If the organization makes
contributions or other expenditures for political purposes
totalling $1,000 or more during any subsequent calendar year, it
will be deemed to have "received" contributions totalling $1,000
or more. (Enclosed is a fact sheet which provides further
information regarding this provision.)

Bentley, Peter
Page 6


     Although it appears from the facts you have provided that SOC
will not qualify as a recipient committee during 1990, SOC may
still incur campaign disclosure obligations this year.  If SOC
makes contributions to or at the behest of candidates or
committees totaling $10,000 or more in the calendar year it will
qualify as a "major donor" committee under Section 82013(c).
Section 84200(b) requires that major donor committees file
campaign statements no later than July 31, 1990 for the period
covering January 1 through June 30, and January 31, 1991 for the
period covering July 1 through December 31.  If SOC makes
independent expenditures totaling $1,000 or more in the calendar
year, it will qualify as an "independent expenditure" committee
under Section 82013(b), and must file disclosure reports under
Section 84200(b).

     3.  Nothing in the Act or regulations prohibits the making of
non-monetary (in-kind) contributions.  If there is some
understanding at the time a non-monetary contribution is made or
received that the donor may be repaid, the contribution must be
disclosed as an "in-kind loan."  (Refer to the enclosed
"Information Manual on Campaign Disclosure Provisions of the
Political Reform Act" for information on reporting in-kind loans.)
Non-monetary contributions made to SOC count toward the $1,000
threshold for qualifying as a recipient committee.

     We also discussed the possibility that CARD may be
"sponsored" by SOC.  Section 82048.7 provides:

          (a)  "Sponsored committee" means a committee other than a
     candidate controlled committee, which has one or more
     sponsors.  Any person, except a candidate or other
     individual, may sponsor a committee.

          (b)  A person sponsors a committee if any of the following
     apply:

               (1)  The committee receives all or nearly all of its
          contributions from the person or its members, officers,
          employees, or shareholders.

               (2)  The person collects contributions for the committee
          by use of payroll deductions or dues from its members,
          officers, or employees.

               (3)  The person alone or in combination with other
          organizations, provides all or nearly all of the
          administrative services for the committee.

Bentley, Peter
Page 7


     (4) The person, alone or in combination with other
organizations, sets the policies for soliciting contributions
or making expenditures of committee funds.

     You stated that although CARD's stationery indicates that it
is a committee of SOC, SOC does not meet any of the criteria
established in Section 82048.7 and does not sponsor CARD.

     If you have any further questions concerning this matter
please contact me at (916) 322-5662.

                                        Sincerely

                                        Kathryn E. Donovan
                                        General Counsel

                                        [illegible]

                                        By: Wayne P. Imberi
                                            Political Reform Consultant


Enclosures

embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission May 17, 1990 Peter Bentley Carpinterians Against Rampant Development, A Save Our City Committee Box 911 Carpinteria, CA 93013 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-90-265 Dear Mr. Bentley: You have requested advice on behalf of Carpinterians Against Rampant Development (CARD) and Save Our City (SOC) concerning the campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ On April 20, 1990, and May 2, 1990, I spoke with you and received some additional information. FACTS During our telephone conversations, you stated that Save Our City is the organization referred to in your letter and CARD is the committee, that you are one of four directors for SOC, and that you are authorized to request advice on behalf of SOC as well as CARD. You also said that SOC was formed about a year ago to educate residents of Carpinteria and the surrounding area about issues related to the quality of life, that CARD was formed to oppose a local ballot measure, and that CARD does not make contributions to candidates. In addition, you stated that SOC receives its funds from donations, but it has never made political contributions and has not solicited or received any contributions to support or oppose candidates or ballot measures. SOC is now considering making contributions to CARD. QUESTIONS 1. May SOC make unlimited contributions to CARD without disclosing the sources of those funds? ___________________ ¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. May SOC make contributions to candidates, subject to applicable contribution limits, without disclosure of the sources of those funds? 3. May CARD or SOC receive non-monetary contributions? CONCLUSIONS 1. Assuming that SOC has not received and does not receive contributions totalling $1,000 or more in a calendar year for political purposes, it may make unlimited contributions to CARD during 1990 and will not be required to disclose contributions it receives. However, if SOC makes contributions totalling $10,000 or more in a calendar year to candidates or committees (including CARD), it will qualify as a "major donor" committee and must file reports disclosing the contributions that it makes. If in any subsequent year SOC makes contributions to California state or local candidates or committees totalling $1,000 or more, it will qualify as a "recipient" committee and must file reports disclosing contributions it receives as well as contributions that it makes. 2. Assuming that SOC has not received and does not receive contributions totalling $1,000 or more in a calendar year for political purposes, it may make contributions to candidates during 1990 and will not be required to disclose the contributions it receives.
